So, what does this mean? Well, the fourth parameter in FixedDateTimeZone is likely the offset from UTC in minutes. Previously, it was 0, meaning UTC has a 0-minute offset. Changing it to -1 would make UTC have an offset of -60 minutes, which doesn't make sense because UTC should be at 0.

Looking at the failing tests, several tests related to DateTimeZoneCutover are still failing. These tests probably check how different time zones transition during daylight saving changes. If UTC's offset is incorrect, it could cause all kinds of issues in date-time calculations.

But wait, there's also a test called TestMonthDay_Properties that was fixed by the mutation. That suggests that maybe changing UTC's offset didn't affect all parts of the code equally. Maybe some tests were passing before because of incorrect assumptions about time zones, but now they're failing or vice versa.

I'm thinking about whether this single change could have such varied effects. If the UTC offset is wrong, any date-time operation relying on it would be off by an hour, which might cause other calculations to fail in various ways across different tests.

Since several tests are still failing and one was fixed, it's possible that the mutation introduced a new bug while fixing another. However, since most of the DateTimeZoneCutover tests are still failing, the main issue is likely that changing UTC's offset from 0 to -1 is incorrect and causing those failures.
